coredata.dc:title: Harmonic responses and cavitation activity of encapsulated microbubbles coupled with magnetic nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Yuyang
Chen, Chuyi
Tu, Juan
Guo, Xiasheng
Wu, Hongyi
Zhang, Dong
coredata.dc:description: Encapsulated microbubbles coupled with magnetic nanoparticles, one kind of hybrid agents that can integrate both ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging/therapy functions, have attracted increasing interests in both research and clinic communities. However, there is a lack of comprehensive understanding of their dynamic behaviors generated in diagnostic and therapeutic applications. In the present work, a hybrid agent was synthesized by integrating superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIOs) into albumin-shelled microbubbles (named as SPIO-albumin microbubbles). Then, both the stable and inertial cavitation thresholds of this hybrid agent were measured at varied SPIO concentrations and ultrasound parameters (e.g., frequency, pressure amplitude, and pulse length). The results show that, at a fixed acoustic driving frequency, both the stable and inertial cavitation thresholds of SPIO-albumin microbubble should decrease with the increasing SPIO concentration and acoustic driving pulse length. The inertial cavitation threshold of SPIO-albumin microbubbles also decreases with the raised driving frequency, while the minimum sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds appear at twice and two thirds resonance frequency, respectively. It is also noticed that both the stable and inertial cavitation thresholds of SonoVue microbubbles are similar to those measured for hybrid microbubbles with a SPIO concentration of 114.7μg/ml. The current work could provide better understanding on the impact of the integrated SPIOs on the dynamic responses (especially the cavitation activities) of hybrid microbubbles, and suggest the shell composition of hybrid agents should be appropriately designed to improve their clinical diagnostic and therapeutic performances of hybrid microbubble agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stable cavitation
Inertial cavitation
Pressure threshold
Hybrid contrast agents
Superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of sub-harmonic and ultra-harmonic thresholds of SC microbubbles
Determination of the IC thresholds of microbubbles
Frequency spectra of microbubbles undergoing stable/inertial cavitation
Sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds measured for SPIO-albumin and SonoVue microbubbles
IC thresholds measured for SPIO-albumin and SonoVue microbubbles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the ultrasound exposure and detection system.

Determination of the sub-harmonic threshold of microbubbles. The sample FFT spectrum is calculated for the bubble excited at a 1-MHz driving frequency, 100-Hz PRF and 20-cycle pulse length. The diluted microbubble volume concentration was 0.25% and the SPIO concentration was 19.6mg/ml.

Determination of the IC threshold of microbubbles. (a) The FFT spectra of the scattered signals detected at P − =30kPa (dash line) and 140kPa (solid line). The frequency, pulse repetition rate and pulse length of the driving signals are 1MHz, 100Hz, and 20 cycles, respectively. (b) IC doses calculated for SPIO-albumin microbubbles and PBS (Phosphate Buffered Saline) as a function of acoustic pressure. The baseline and IC threshold are defined in the text. The diluted microbubble volume concentration was 0.25% and the SPIO concentration was 19.6μg/ml.

The FFT spectra obtained at different acoustic driving pressures. (a) P − =10kPa; no harmonic components are apparent. (b) P− =30kPa; harmonic components appear. (c) P − =60kPa; sub- and ultra-harmonic components appear. (d) P − =140kPa; the broadband noise level increases and the sub- and ultra-harmonic components are suppressed.

Pulse-length-dependence of the (a) sub- and (b) ultra-harmonic thresholds for SonoVue microbubbles and SPIO-encapsulated microbubbles at different SPIO concentrations. The acoustic driving frequency was 1MHz and the volume concentration of the diluted microbubble suspensions was 0.25%.

Frequency-dependence of the (a) sub- and (b) ultra-harmonic thresholds for SonoVue microbubbles and SPIO-encapsulated microbubbles at different SPIO concentrations. The pulse length was 20 cycles and the volume concentration of the diluted microbubble suspensions was 0.25%. The resonance frequencies (f 0) of different concentration of SPIO microbubbles and SonoVue microbubbles are 3.9MHz, 3.6MHz, 3.3MHz, 2.9MHz and 3.1MHz, respectively.

Simulated frequency-dependence of the (a) sub- and (b) ultra-harmonic thresholds for microbubbles with the increasing shell elasticity. The initial bubble diameter was set to be 3μm. The acoustic pulse length was fixed at 20 cycles and the volume concentration of the diluted microbubble suspensions was 0.25%. The resonance frequencies (f 0) of different concentration of SPIO microbubbles and SonoVue microbubbles are 3.9MHz, 3.6MHz, 3.3MHz, 2.9MHz, and 3.1MHz, respectively.

IC thresholds measured for SonoVue microbubbles and SPIO-albumin microbubbles with different SPIO concentrations. (a) IC thresholds were measured as the function of pulse length. The acoustic frequency was fixed at 1MHz. (b) IC thresholds were measured at varied acoustic driving frequency. The acoustic pulse length was 20 cycles.

head.ce:title: Harmonic responses and cavitation activity of encapsulated microbubbles coupled with magnetic nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Acoustics (MOE), Department of Physics, Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Microstructure, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Microstructure

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The State Key Laboratory of Acoustics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing 10080, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The State Key Laboratory of Acoustics

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Acoustics, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Encapsulated microbubbles coupled with magnetic nanoparticles, one kind of hybrid agents that can integrate both ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging/therapy functions, have attracted increasing interests in both research and clinic communities. However, there is a lack of comprehensive understanding of their dynamic behaviors generated in diagnostic and therapeutic applications. In the present work, a hybrid agent was synthesized by integrating superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIOs) into albumin-shelled microbubbles (named as SPIO-albumin microbubbles). Then, both the stable and inertial cavitation thresholds of this hybrid agent were measured at varied SPIO concentrations and ultrasound parameters (e.g., frequency, pressure amplitude, and pulse length). The results show that, at a fixed acoustic driving frequency, both the stable and inertial cavitation thresholds of SPIO-albumin microbubble should decrease with the increasing SPIO concentration and acoustic driving pulse length. The inertial cavitation threshold of SPIO-albumin microbubbles also decreases with the raised driving frequency, while the minimum sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds appear at twice and two thirds resonance frequency, respectively. It is also noticed that both the stable and inertial cavitation thresholds of SonoVue microbubbles are similar to those measured for hybrid microbubbles with a SPIO concentration of 114.7μg/ml. The current work could provide better understanding on the impact of the integrated SPIOs on the dynamic responses (especially the cavitation activities) of hybrid microbubbles, and suggest the shell composition of hybrid agents should be appropriately designed to improve their clinical diagnostic and therapeutic performances of hybrid microbubble agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrasound contrast agent (UCA) microbubbles (microbubbles) are usually insoluble gas core (e.g., perfluorocarbon and sulfur hexafluoride) encapsulated by thin coatings (e.g., lipid, albumin and polymer) that can prevent bubbles from quick dissolution. Taking advantages from the compressible gas cores and resonant-induced intense scattering, UCA microbubbles injected into the bloodstream were initially utilized in diagnostic ultrasound (US) imaging to enhance the acoustic contrast of specific cardiographic or radiologic features. With more popular use of UCA microbubbles, their potential therapeutic applications relevant to US-induced bioeffects were also investigated both in vitro and in vivo [1–3]. More recently, due to the increasing demands of performance improvement of UCAs, hybrid contrast agent microbubbles, which can integrate more than one diagnostic/therapeutic modalities in one agent, have attracted broad interests in both research and clinic communities [4–6].

Superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIOs) are one kind of powerful multifunctional agents that can both effectively enhance magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast and potentially serve as magnetic nonviral vectors [7–9]. It has been reported in previous literatures that, by integrating SPIOs into the shells of UCA microbubbles, hybrid US/MRI contrast agents can be fabricated to achieve higher imaging resolution, deeper tissue penetration depth, better treatment efficacy and greater clinic safety [10–12]. However, the embedding of SPIOs to microbubble shell structures will also significantly affect the dynamic responses of the microbubbles, e.g., the generation of harmonics and US-induced cavitation activities [13–15].

It is well accepted that US-induced stable cavitation (SC) and inertial cavitation (IC) play an important role in the development of microbubble-assisted diagnostic and therapeutic applications, e.g., contrast harmonic imaging, low intensity pulsed US stimulated fracture healing, high intensity focused US cancer therapy, and US-induced gene/drug delivery [16–18]. It has been reported that the SC of UCAs is usually determined by sub-harmonic and ultra-harmonic responses, which vary with acoustic parameters such as the pulse length, amplitude, and frequency [19,20]. Other studies have also been performed to investigate the IC thresholds of UCAs [21–25]. However, up to our knowledge, currently it is lack of better understanding of how the embedding of SPIOs into the shells of UCA microbubbles affects the cavitation threshold and dose of the hybrid US/MRI contrast agents.

In the present work, hybrid US/MRI contrast agent microbubbles were synthesized by embedding SPIOs to albumin-shelled perfluorocarbon microbubbles (referred as SPIO-albumin microbubbles for short). Then, both the SC and IC doses generated by the SPIO-albumin microbubbles were quantitatively measured based on passive cavitation detection (PCD) method. As the results, the SC and IC thresholds of SPIO-albumin microbubbles could be determined at different SPIO concentrations and US parameters (e.g., frequency, pressure amplitude, and pulse length). For comparison, the same measurements were applied to commercially available SonoVue microbubbles as well. This work will be helpful for better understanding how the integration of SPIOs into the shells of UCAs plays an important impact on the microbubble dynamic response (especially their cavitation activities), which should be beneficial for improving the development of hybrid contrast agents and providing new insights for their diagnostic/therapeutic applications, while minimizing undesired side-effects (e.g., vascular endothelial damage, cell apoptosis and hemorrhage) [26–28].

The hybrid contrast agent used in the present work was synthesized by loading SPIOs into albumin-shelled perfluorocarbon microbubbles. The preparing of SPIO-albumin-shelled perfluorocarbon microbubbles involves two steps: synthesis of the SPIOs and assembly of the SPIO-albumin microbubbles. A brief description of the method is as follows. An aqueous solution of FeCl3·6H2O (1.0mol/L, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) and FeSO4·7H2O (0.5mol/L, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was used as the iron source. Concentrated NH3·H2O (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was added to the iron-containing solution until the pH reached 11.0 under a nitrogen atmosphere to prevent oxidation. Oleic acid (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was then added to the alkaline solution to yield mono-disperse hydrophobic Fe3O4-oleic acid nanoparticles. Via a surface double-exchange reaction between oleic acid and meso-2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA; Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), mono-disperse, water-soluble SPIOs (Fe3O4-DMSA) were obtained and collected through a magnetic separation procedure. After pH neutralization, the SPIO solution was dialyzed through dialysis membranes (Cellu-Sep T3, Membrane Filtration Products Inc., Seguin, Texas, USA) in pure water for 3days to remove excess impurities. The final sample was filtrated through a 0.22-μm membrane and stored at 4°C. In the second step, a sonication method, similar to that reported by Porter et al. [29], was used to synthesize the SPIO-albumin microbubbles. Briefly, 10% bovine serum albumin and 60% sucrose were mixed with a volume ratio of 1:1 in deionized water. A certain amount of SPIOs were then added to the mixed solution. The mixed solution was placed and kept overnight in a bell-like glass chamber (radius: 9cm; height: 20cm) saturated with perfluorocarbon (C3F8). The solution was then sonicated with a horn-type ultrasonic processor (VCX750, Sonics and Materials Inc., Newtown, CT, USA) for 2min, while a syringe pump (LEGATO 270, KD Scientific Inc., Holliston, MA, USA) was used to continuously pump in C3F8 gas. The ultrasonic processor worked in pulsed mode with an on/off ratio of 3:1, a working frequency of 20kHz, and an intensity of 300W. The SPIO-albumin microbubbles were centrifuged and then washed to eliminate free SPIOs. Finally, the upper layer of the multibubble suspensions was collected for subsequent analysis.

For comparison, a commercially available UCA (SonoVue, Bracco Diagnostics Inc., Geneva, Switzerland) was studied also. SonoVue microbubbles consist of sulfur hexafluoride (SF6) gas encapsulated by a thin lipid monolayer membrane. The microbubbles were reconstituted according to the manufacturer’s instructions by venting the sample vial with a sterile 18-gauge needle, followed by a 5-mL injection of phosphate buffered saline into the vial. The vial contained 2–5×108 microbubbles/mL and through inversion agitation the microbubbles were evenly distributed. The reconstituted SonoVue suspension was diluted for analysis.

The experimental apparatus are illustrated in Fig. 1 . Tone-burst signals were generated by an arbitrary waveform generator (33250A, Agilent, Santa Clara, CA). While being amplified by a 53-dB power amplifier (2200L, E&I, Rochester, NY), signals with different parameters (e.g., frequencies, pulse lengths and pressures) were used to drive four unfocused transducers. The central frequencies of the transducers #1 to #4 were 1, 2.25, 3.5 and 5MHz, respectively (V303-SU, V325-SU, V383-SU and V326-SU, Panametrics, Waltham, MA). The transmitted ultrasound waves were sonicated at a 1.5-mL hydrophilic polyester sample tube (1-cm diameter, 30-μm wall thickness; Vention Medical, Salem, NH) filled with diluted microbubble suspensions. The peak negative pressures generated by the source transducers were calibrated with a needle hydrophone (HNC-0100, Onda Corporation, Sunnyvale, CA) that was controlled by a three-dimensional positioning system (Newport, Irvine, CA) using the LABVIEW software (NI Corporation, TX), which allowed the peak negative acoustic driving pressure to be determined from the input electrical voltages.

To determine the SC and IC threshold of different microbubbles, the output voltage of the function generator was increased with a 5-mV step size every 2s from the initial level (viz., 0mV) over a 60-s period for each sample. During the experiment, in order to reduce the impact of the source signal on the detection of the broadband noise and sub- and ultra-harmonic signals, the needle hydrophone was positioned 90° to the source to detect the scattered and emitted signals from the microbubbles. The detected signals were digitalized by and displayed on a digital oscilloscope (54830B, Agilent, Santa Clara, CA) with a sampling frequency of 25MHz; each signal contained 20,000 data points. For each experiment, five signals were measured with fresh microbubble suspensions. For microbubbles in individual series (e.g., SonoVue microbubbles and hybrid microbubbles with various SPIO concentrations), five replicate measurements were performed for sample suspensions withdrawn from different vials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of sub-harmonic and ultra-harmonic thresholds of SC microbubbles
ce:section.ce:para: Normally, when the microbubbles were excited by US pulses, SC will occur first as the acoustic driving pressure reaches a certain threshold. When the driving pressure increases further to exceed a higher threshold, IC takes place. The scattered and emitted signals can be detected by a passive cavitation detection (PCD) system and used to determine these two cavitation thresholds, which are characterized by a sudden rise in the intensity of the frequency spectrum. The most obvious features are the appearance of sub- or ultra-harmonic components and the enhanced level of broadband noises.

It is known that both sub- and ultra-harmonics can be observed when microbubbles are undergoing SC motion. Therefore, the sub-harmonic and ultra-harmonic thresholds of SC microbubbles were both determined here based on the method introduced in the work done by Vignon et al. [30,31]. To determine the threshold of the sub-harmonic component, the FFT spectrum of the bubble oscillation signals were calculated first. If the local sub-harmonic (0.5f 0) amplitude exceeds the amplitudes of the noise signals at 0.25f 0 and 0.75f 0 by 3dB, the temporal exciting acoustic pressure is regarded as the threshold of the sub-harmonic component (as shown in Fig. 2 ). Similarly, the ultra-harmonic pressure threshold could be determined when the local ultra-harmonic (1.5f 0) amplitude is at least higher than the amplitudes of the noises at 1.25f 0 and 1.75f 0 by 3dB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the IC thresholds of microbubbles
ce:section.ce:para: The IC dosimetry method used here is similar to that described in more detail elsewhere [32,33]. Briefly, Fast Fourier transform (FFT) was performed on each recorded waveform and the root-mean-square (RMS) amplitude of the broadband noise was determined within a specific narrow frequency window (0.5MHz) between the third and fourth harmonics. The RMS amplitudes were then plotted as functions of time and the accumulated IC dose was quantified as the integrated area under the curve over the entire time period sampled.

The IC doses were then plotted as functions of the calibrated acoustic pressure (P −), as shown in Fig. 3 . All results were presented as the means±standard deviations for 5 replicate measurements. The IC threshold is defined as pressure level at which the IC dose of the sample microbubbles is statistically higher than the baseline; the baseline was measured in degassed phosphate-buffered saline without microbubbles. Thus, a single-tailed Student’s t-test was adopted to statistically compare the IC doses generated under different conditions with the baseline, and the statistic significance was accepted at p <0.05. The IC dosimetry and statistic analysis methods used here were described in more detail elsewhere [32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Frequency spectra of microbubbles undergoing stable/inertial cavitation
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, individual SonoVue and SPIO-albumin microbubble samples were sonicated to ultrasound pulses with varied exciting pressures. At the initial stage, since the acoustic driving pressure is quite low (e.g., P − =10kPa) the microbubbles only undergo linear oscillations so that only the fundamental frequency could be observed in the spectrum (Fig. 4 a). With the increase of the exciting pressure, the fundamental and harmonic components increased in intensity, which corresponds the enhancement of microbubble oscillation amplitudes (Fig. 4b) When the exciting acoustic pressure increases above a certain threshold (e.g., P − =60kPa), the microbubbles will exhibit stable cavitation phenomenon, so that both sub- and ultra-harmonic components appear in the spectrum (Fig. 4c). As the acoustic pressure is further raised to a relatively high level (e.g., P − =140kPa), broadband noise generated from the microbubbles undergoing violent IC activity dominates the frequency spectrum, and the sub- and ultra-harmonic components become inconspicuous or even disappear (Fig. 4d). All the above observations indicate that the variation of the cavitation activities generated by hybrid microbubble should be highly dependent on the acoustic driving pressure, which is similar to the behavior of regular UCA microbubbles reported in previous papers [30,31,24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds measured for SPIO-albumin and SonoVue microbubbles
ce:section.ce:para: It has been shown that, when the acoustic driving pressures exceed a certain threshold, both sub- and ultra-harmonic components can be significantly observed in the spectrum, which indicate the generation of microbubble stable cavitation activity. Here, we are interested in how the sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds are affected by the integration of SPIOs into the microbubble shells and the acoustic driving parameters. Therefore, in the present work, the sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds of SonoVue microbubbles and SPIO-albumin microbubbles with different SPIO concentrations (viz., 0, 19.6, 114.7, and 292.0μg/ml) were measured at varied acoustic driving frequency or pulse length.

The acoustic pulse length is one of the most important parameters that affect the clinical applications of microbubbles. For diagnostic ultrasound, shorter pulses are usually adopted, whereas for therapeutic applications, longer pulses are preferred. The experimental results plotted in Fig. 5 show that, for a fixed acoustic driving frequency, both the sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds decrease with the increasing SPIO concentration as well as the increasing pulse length, although they tend to reach a saturate value when the pulse length is longer than 20 cycles. It is noteworthy that the measured sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds are comparable to each other, which suggests either the sub- or the ultra-harmonic threshold can be regarded as the indicator of the SC threshold of the microbubbles.

The acoustic driving frequency is another important factor that can influence the microbubble dynamic responses. It is well known the microbubble oscillation amplitude tends to reach the maximum level when the acoustic driving frequency is as close to the microbubble resonance frequency (f 0) as possible. It is well known that the microbubble resonance frequency is dependent on its radius and shell parameters [22]. Previous studies have reported that, when the SPIO concentration was raised from 0 to 292.0μg/ml, the mean diameters of SPIO-albumin microbubbles with different SPIO concentration were measured to be 2.6μm, 2.7μm, 2.9μm, 3.5μm, respectively [14], and the mean diameter of SonoVue microbubbles was measured to be around 1.9μm [25]. Thus, the corresponding resonance frequencies of SPIO-albumin microbubbles with different SPIO concentration and SonoVue microbubbles can be estimated to be about 3.9MHz, 3.6MHz, 3.3MHz, 2.9MHz, and 3.1MHz [14,22]. Thus, four transducers, whose central frequencies are 1.0, 2.25, 3.5 and 5.0MHz were used to cover the range of microbubble resonance frequencies. Here, for microbubbles with different shell compositions, the dependences of their sub- and ultra-harmonic pressure thresholds on varied excitation frequencies normalized by individual resonance frequencies (f/f 0) are illustrated in Fig. 6 . Similar to the results shown in Fig. 5, at a particular normalized excitation frequency, the measured sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds are of comparable magnitude, and the enhancement of SPIO concentration in the bubble shells will result in a decrease in the microbubble SC threshold (both the sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds). Moreover, it is also noticed in Fig. 6 that the SC threshold of the SonoVue microbubbles is close to that of hybrid microbubbles with a SPIO concentration of 114.7μg/ml. According to data reported by previous work, the shell stiffness parameters of SPIO-albumin microbubbles (3-μm diameter) with varied SPIO concentrations decreased from ∼0.16 to 0.10N/m, as the SPIO concentration increased from 0 to 292.0μg/ml [14]. Based on the encapsulated microbubble dynamic models (e.g., Hoff’s model), the radius–time (R–T) curves and corresponding FFT spectra can be simulated for different kinds of microbubbles [25]. Therefore, the frequency-dependence of the microbubble sub- and ultra-thresholds was also numerically estimated, and Fig. 7 demonstrates that the simulated results are qualitatively consistent with the experimental observations.

However, different from the monotonously decreased trend demonstrated for the pulse-length-dependence of microbubble SC threshold (see Fig. 5), a nonlinear variation of microbubble sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds can be observed when the normalized excitation frequency increases (see Figs. 6 and 7). Generally, a local minimum can be observed for both sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds at ∼2f 0 (Fig. 6a) and ∼2/3f 0 (Fig. 6b), respectively. The simulated results shown in Fig. 7 also confirm the minimum sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds should appear around 2f 0 and 2/3f 0. The most possible explanation for this phenomenon is that microbubbles always tend to exhibit strongest oscillations at its natural resonance frequency. For instance, at a excitation frequency of 2/3f 0, the corresponding ultra-harmonic right equals to the resonance frequency f 0, so that the strongest ultra-harmonic oscillations should be achieved which gives the lowest ultra-harmonic pressure threshold [35]. For the same reason, the minimum sub-harmonic threshold should also be expected at 2f 0. However, it should be noticed that, besides 2f 0 and 2/3f 0, another local minimum can also be observed in both Figs. 6 and 7, especially for the sub-harmonic thresholds estimated for albumin-shelled microbubbles and hybrid microbubbles with a SPIO concentration of 19.6mg/ml (Fig. 7a). Actually, Katiyar and Sarkar have claimed that the minimum threshold for the generation of microbubble sub-harmonics is not always appears at 2f 0; instead it occurs over a relatively broad frequency range (0.6⩽ f/f 0 ⩽2.2) within which the sub-harmonic thresholds are of comparable magnitude and much smaller than the magnitude outside the frequency band [20]. They have also pointed out that the quantitative variation of frequency-dependent sub-harmonic threshold could be strongly dependent on the specific parameters adopted in the bubble dynamic model (e.g., varied bubble radius and radius-dependent shell surface properties). Meanwhile, in order to simplify the calculation complexity, the bubble–bubble interaction was ignored during the simulation procedures, which could be considered as another possible reason for the variation of the frequency point where the lowest threshold appears [36].

Another point one may noticed here is the simulated SC thresholds of the hybrid microbubbles are generally higher than the measured ones. Previous studies have suggested that the IC thresholds of UCAs would be significantly reduced by increasing the microbubble volume concentration, because there are more microbubbles could act as nucleation sites to enable the generation of IC activities [21,25,33,24,36]. Although the volume concentration of the diluted microbubble suspensions was set to be 0.25% for both experiments and simulations, some daughter bubbles might be generated by the collapsed cavitation bubbles during the experimental measurements, while the volume concentration was kept constant and the bubble–bubble interactions were also ignored during the simulation processes. Therefore, it is reasonable to observe a discrepancy in the magnitude of SC thresholds between the measured and simulated results, and lower SC thresholds should be expected for the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IC thresholds measured for SPIO-albumin and SonoVue microbubbles
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that broadband noise emission can be regarded as an indicator for the presence of violent IC activity [24]. There are several ideas have been put forward on the original mechanisms of the broadband noise. One is the temporal fluctuation emitted from bubbles, the other is the acoustic waves radiated from non-periodically pulsating bubbles [34], and the shock waves emitted by collapsing bubbles [37]. In the present work, the impacts of SPIO concentration and acoustic driving parameters on the IC thresholds of hybrid microbubbles with different SPIO concentrations were also investigated, based on the detection of IC-induced broadband noise signals and the quantification of IC doses. Meanwhile, the IC threshold of SonoVue microbubbles was also measured for comparison.

As shown in Fig. 8 a, with the same SPIO concentration, a greater number of acoustic driving cycles are required for lowering the IC threshold, because more acoustic energy will be delivered with the longer driving pulses. This observation is consistent with previous reports regarding the regular UCA microbubbles [23–25]. The frequency-dependence of IC threshold is plotted for the microbubbles with different shell compositions in Fig. 8b. Due to the limitation of the transducer resources, the variation of IC thresholds was only measured for the microbubbles excited at a frequency of 1.0, 2.0, 2.25 and 3.0MHz. The results indicate that, for a fixed SPIO concentration, the IC threshold of microbubbles can be reduced by increasing the acoustic driving frequency from 1.0 to 3.0MHz. The reason for this phenomenon lies in the resonant oscillations of the bubbles. As mentioned above, the resonance frequencies of hybrid microbubbles and SonoVue microbubbles tested in the current work are estimated to be 3.9MHz, 3.6MHz, 3.3MHz, 2.9MHz and 3.1MHz, respectively. In other words, the resonant frequencies of all the microbubble samples are either around or slightly beyond 3MHz. Therefore, it is easier to stimulate stronger microbubble oscillations and cavitation activities with an excitation frequency of 3MHz rather than lower frequencies.

Another observation illustrated in Fig. 8 is that, for constant acoustic driving parameters, the IC threshold of SPIO-albumin microbubbles decreases with the increasing SPIO concentration, which is similar to the trend observed for the SC threshold (i.e., both the sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds). As reported in previous literatures, there are several ways to reduce the cavitation threshold. One is the addition of particles or microbubbles to provide more cavitation nuclei [25,38,39]. The other way is to change the mechanical properties of microbubbles (e.g., bubbles size and shell visco-elastic parameters). It has been shown that the increase in the bubble radius or the decrease in the bubble effective elasticity or resonance frequency could significantly lower the microbubble cavitation threshold [20,24,25]. Guo et al. reported that the lipid-shelled SonoVue microbubbles have a lower IC threshold than the albumin-shelled KangRun microbubbles [25], because the SonoVue microbubbles have a thinner lipid shells with relatively lowered shell elasticity and viscosity. The recent study regarding the mechanical properties of SPIO-albumin microbubbles also revealed that, with more SPIOs integrated into the microbubble shells, the mean radius of SPIO-albumin microbubbles would be enlarged and the effective microbubble stiffness could be decreased, which in turn results in an enhanced cavitation intensity and a weakened resistance to microbubble collapse [14]. As the results, it is reasonable to expect the SC and IC thresholds of SPIO-albumin microbubbles would be lowered by the increasing SPIO concentration in the bubble shells. It should also be noticed that both the SC and IC thresholds of SonoVue microbubbles are comparable to those of the SPIO-albumin microbubbles with a SPIO concentration of 114.7μg/ml (see Figs. 6–8), which suggests that the dynamic characteristics of SPIO-albumin microbubbles with 114.7μg/ml SPIO concentration are quite similar to those of SonoVue microbubbles.

In summary, the aim of the current study is to investigate how the dynamic characteristics of hybrid SPIO-albumin microbubbles, especially their SC and IC thresholds, are affected by the integration of SPIO nanoparticles in the UCA microbubble shells and the acoustic driving parameters (e.g., acoustic driving frequency and pulse length). The results show that the sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds of both SPIO-albumin microbubbles and SonoVue microbubbles are comparable to each other, so that either one can be selected to represent the SC threshold of microbubbles. At a fixed SPIO concentration, the SC thresholds of SPIO-albumin microbubbles will decrease with the increasing acoustic driving pulse length, while the minimum sub- and ultra-harmonic thresholds appear at 2f 0 and 2/3f 0, respectively. It is more interesting to observe that both the SC and IC thresholds of SonoVue microbubbles are similar to those measured for hybrid microbubbles with a SPIO concentration of 114.7μg/ml, and both the SC and IC thresholds of hybrid microbubbles could be lowered with more SPIO nanoparticles embedding into the microbubble shells. The above observations suggest that, in practical design and applications of hybrid MRI/US microbubbles, appropriate SPIO concentration should be adopted to synthesize desired hybrid MRI/US microbubbles to satisfy the particular diagnostic/therapeutic requirements.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Basic Research Program 973 (Grant no. 2011CB707900), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no’s. 81127901, 81227004, 11374155, 11174141, 11274170, 11274176, 11474001, 11474161, and 11474166), Nature Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK2011812), The Six Top Talents of Jiangsu Province of China (No. 2010-WS-082), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 020414380015) and the National High-Tech Research and Development Program 863 (2012AA022702).
